plainMeaning: Of everything good this is the most blessed, the root of all welfare and the highest of all paths — so victory ever belongs to the Jina's teaching.
recitation:
Sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ, sarva-kalyāṇa-kāraṇaṁ |
Pradhānaṁ sarva-dharmāṇāṁ, jainaṁ jayati śāsanam ||

wordByWord.gloss: (among) all auspicious things; everything that is a blessing
wordByWord.etymology: Tatpuruṣa of sarva ('all') + maṅgala ('the auspicious, a blessing,' from √maṅg, 'to move/advance, prosper').
wordByWord.notes: Read in apposition with the following word as sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ — 'the auspiciousness of all auspicious things.'

wordByWord.gloss: the (highest) auspiciousness; that which makes auspicious
wordByWord.etymology: Abstract derivative (vṛddhi + suffix -ya) of maṅgala; nominative/accusative neuter singular predicating the śāsana.
wordByWord.notes: The vṛddhi superlative force: not merely auspicious but the very essence of auspiciousness.

wordByWord.gloss: the cause of all welfare / of every blessing (ultimately, of liberation)
wordByWord.etymology: sarva ('all') + kalyāṇa ('welfare, good fortune,' from √kal) + kāraṇa ('cause,' from √kṛ); neuter sg.
wordByWord.notes: In Jain usage kalyāṇa also connotes the spiritual good culminating in mokṣa.

wordByWord.gloss: the foremost, the chief, the principal one
wordByWord.etymology: pra- ('forth, before') + √dhā ('to place') — 'placed in front,' hence pre-eminent; neuter sg. agreeing with śāsana.

wordByWord.gloss: of all dharmas / of all spiritual paths and duties
wordByWord.etymology: sarva + dharma (√dhṛ, 'to uphold'); genitive plural — 'among all dharmas.'
wordByWord.notes: Partitive genitive governed by pradhānaṁ: 'chief among all dharmas.'

wordByWord.gloss: the Jaina (teaching); that which pertains to the Jina
wordByWord.etymology: vṛddhi derivative of Jina ('the victor,' √ji, 'to conquer') — 'belonging to the Jina'; neuter sg. qualifying śāsana.
√ji ('to conquer'), present indicative 3rd sg. (Pāṇinian bhū-class) — durative present: 'goes on conquering.'
wordByWord.notes: The lone finite verb; its present tense asserts an ongoing, not a one-time, triumph.

wordByWord.gloss: the teaching, dispensation, order (the living tradition of the Jina)
wordByWord.etymology: √śās ('to instruct, govern') + suffix -ana — 'instruction, rule, doctrine'; neuter sg., grammatical subject of jayati.
wordByWord.notes: Here the doctrine-and-community as a whole, not a building.

literalTranslation: The auspiciousness of all auspicious things, the cause of all welfare, the foremost of all dharmas — the Jaina teaching is victorious.
idiomaticTranslation: Most blessed of all that is blessed, wellspring of every good, supreme among all paths of duty — victory, unceasing victory, to the teaching of the Jina!

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the preceding verse has affirmed 'that alone is true which the Jinas proclaimed,' this closing gāthā does the natural next thing: having accepted the Jina's word as truth, it proclaims that word supreme and salutes its triumph. The structure is a rising fourfold superlative. First a self-referential intensifier — māṅgalya 'of all auspicious things' (sarva-maṅgala): the teaching is not one blessing among many but the auspiciousness in which every other blessing participates. Second, sarva-kalyāṇa-kāraṇaṁ grounds that claim causally: it is the source of all welfare, worldly and, decisively, the spiritual welfare ending in liberation. Third, pradhānaṁ sarva-dharmāṇāṁ ranks it first 'among all dharmas.' The single finite verb jayati then gathers the three epithets into one ringing predicate. Its present tense is the heart of the verse: not 'may it conquer' (an optative wish) but 'it is conquering' — a victory understood as perpetually in force. So the practitioner ends the sāmāyika not with a petition but with a confident declaration, sealing the whole conclusion on a note of joy rather than of confession.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse is a maṅgala — an auspicious benediction whose function is to remove obstacles and consecrate the act just completed. Its boldness ('chief of all dharmas') is doctrinal rather than merely sectarian: the Jaina path of ahiṁsā, anekānta and self-conquest is held to be the one that actually severs karma and yields mokṣa, hence kalyāṇa-kāraṇa in the fullest sense. Note that śāsana denotes the Jina's living dispensation — doctrine, order and community together — so 'victory to the śāsana' is the wish that this whole transmission endure. The first hemistich deliberately echoes a benedictory formula known across Indian devotion (cf. the Śākta 'sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalye śive…'); the Jaina redaction keeps the auspicious opening but redirects the praise to the jaina-śāsana.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited as the very last line, it lets the reciter step out of the vow of equanimity carrying conviction and gladness rather than anxiety over the faults just confessed. Chanted aloud it is a daily reaffirmation of refuge in the teaching, and a reminder that the equanimity practiced for the last forty-eight minutes belongs to a tradition the speaker trusts as supreme.

crossReferences.source: Devī-māhātmya (Durgā-saptaśatī) 11.10 — cited only as a phrasal parallel, not as a Jain source

crossReferences.text: 'jayati' maṅgala formulae closing stotras (e.g. the recurrent jaya-refrains of Jaina stava literature)

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit–English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Root-forms and glosses for māṅgalya, kalyāṇa, pradhāna, śāsana, and √ji (jayati).

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipakṣa edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim wording and placement of the closing maṅgala as Sūtra item (9) of the Sāmāyika Pārvānī.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary text repository (jainqq.org)
sources.consultedFor: Confirmation of 'jainaṁ jayati śāsanam' as a traditional cross-sectarian Jain maṅgala benediction of unfixed single authorship.

notes: The verse is in clear classical Sanskrit and well attested, so confidence is high. Per the preface, authorship is tradition-level (a maṅgala of uncertain single origin), not pinned to a critical edition. The first hemistich shares phrasing with the widely transmitted Śākta verse 'sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalye śive sarvārtha-sādhike'; the Jaina form is best read as an adaptation that keeps the auspicious opening and substitutes a second half praising the jaina-śāsana — noted as a parallel, not a derivation claim. The preceding context verse here is the 'Tameva saccaṁ…' affirmation (Sūtra item 8); no following verse. Tokens are glossed verbatim from the Gujarati source; sarvamaṅgala and māṅgalyaṁ, though printed as two words, are construed together as a single appositional phrase.
